name: OpsDesk
heroDeemphasis: — a self-hosted, multi-tenant support-ops console: tickets, orders, incidents, SLA, and RBAC in one Next.js 16 app over Supabase.
overview: OpsDesk is a single Next.js 16 / React 19 application that gives a support team one place to manage tickets, orders, customers, and incidents — with an SLA/escalation engine, a trigger-based automation engine, org-scoped RBAC, executive reporting, and a separate customer-facing portal — all on top of a single Supabase project instead of a bundled third-party helpdesk SaaS.
contextParagraphs: Based on the feature set actually implemented, OpsDesk reads as a self-hosted alternative to a bundled helpdesk/support-ops SaaS for a team that wants that stack on its own Supabase project rather than a third-party subscription. That is an inference from the code, not a stated product brief — there is no product spec or marketing page in the repository to confirm it.

Two things about the architecture are easy to miss on a first pass: Postgres Row Level Security is enabled on only 3 of roughly 44 application tables (the three purely user-scoped auth tables — passkeys, passkey challenges, email-MFA challenges); every ticket/order/customer/incident/RBAC/audit table instead relies entirely on application code remembering to filter by organization_id. And three separate identity mechanisms coexist by design: a NextAuth JWT session for staff (bridged from Supabase Auth), the underlying Supabase Auth session itself, and a fully independent, hand-rolled magic-link session system for the customer portal.

roleRows.text: Built the application end-to-end as the sole contributor — one git author across all 25 commits, same identity under two name/email-casing variants — domain model, UI, and every Route Handler API under app/api/**.
Designed the multi-tenant data model (organization-scoped tickets, orders, customers, incidents, and roughly 44 tables total) and its application-layer tenant-isolation pattern.
Built the SLA/escalation engine and the trigger → condition → action automation engine, spanning tickets, orders, customers, incidents, and portal events.
Implemented the full auth stack: NextAuth v5 bridged to Supabase Auth, Google OAuth, passwordless magic links, WebAuthn passkeys, and an email-code MFA step-up.

Built org-scoped RBAC (four system roles plus custom roles with permission overrides, optional approval-request workflow), the executive-reporting/analytics views, and a separate passwordless customer portal.

architectureDecisions.title: NextAuth v5 (beta) layered on top of Supabase Auth, instead of either system alone
architectureDecisions.body: The trade-off was giving up a single source of truth for "is this user logged in" — a NextAuth JWT session and an independent Supabase Auth session now both exist per user, and the code has to remember which one to tear down on failure — worth it because it gets Supabase's maintained password/OAuth/magic-link/admin-user machinery for free while every route still gets NextAuth's App-Router-native auth()/useSession() ergonomics, with the org-membership-suspension check centralized in exactly one place (assertHasActiveMembership) across all three credential providers instead of duplicated per sign-in method.

architectureDecisions.title: Shared-schema multi-tenancy with application-layer organization_id filtering, not Postgres RLS
architectureDecisions.body: The trade-off was that tenant isolation became a property of code discipline rather than the database — a single missing .eq("organization_id", ...) filter in any future query is a cross-tenant data leak with no second line of defense, and RLS is enabled on only 3 of the ~44 tables (the auth-only ones, keyed by user_id, not organization_id) — worth it as the cheapest model to build against: ordinary SQL joins across tickets/orders/customers work without per-tenant connection routing, and it's consistent with how the app already resolves the active organization everywhere else (a cookie plus a live membership lookup).

architectureDecisions.title: Redux Toolkit for exactly three global slices (auth, tickets, topbar), local state everywhere else
architectureDecisions.body: The trade-off was inconsistency — a reader can't assume "global state = source of truth" project-wide since orders, customers, incidents, automation, and reports pages all fetch with local component state instead — worth it because the three slices that did get the global-store treatment need explicit, hand-choreographed cross-slice behavior (wiping the ticket cache specifically on an org switch, optimistic patches to cached ticket details without a refetch) that a query-key-based cache library would need bespoke glue to express.

architectureDecisions.title: SSE-shaped server polling for the notification stream, instead of the WebSocket the app already uses elsewhere
architectureDecisions.body: The trade-off was efficiency — the server re-queries Supabase on a fixed 5-second cadence per open browser tab whether or not anything changed, which is strictly less efficient than the real Supabase Realtime WebSocket subscription the same codebase already uses for membership-suspension detection — worth it (as the codebase evidences it) because a long-lived EventSource with hash-diffing and built-in auto-reconnect was simpler to stand up for this feature than wiring a second client-side Realtime subscription, at the cost of now having three different "live update" approaches in the app with no shared rationale between them.

architectureDecisions.title: Client-side, layout-level route protection instead of middleware.ts
architectureDecisions.body: The trade-off was that page protection only ever runs after the page's JS has already been requested and started rendering, so the real security boundary has to be each API route's own auth() check rather than a single edge-level chokepoint — worth it because the entire page-level gate is readable in one client file (a route Set plus a handful of prefix checks) driven by the same useSession() hook the rest of the UI already depends on, instead of maintaining a second, edge-runtime-safe way of reading the session.

measuredQuality.a11yDetail: No axe-core/pa11y test suite and no project-authored eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y config exist — both appear in package-lock.json only as transitive dependencies of eslint-config-next. eslint.config.mjs extends eslint-config-next's core-web-vitals preset, which resolves to enabling six jsx-a11y rules (alt-text, aria-props, aria-proptypes, aria-unsupported-elements, role-has-required-aria-props, role-supports-aria-props) — confirmed by grepping the installed eslint-config-next/dist/index.js. A repo-wide search for real JSX aria-xxx={...} attributes (not Tailwind aria- variant selectors like aria-invalid:) found 8 files under app/.

resultsParagraph: OpsDesk has no production deployment, so there is no real usage, uptime, or field-performance data to report. What's actually verifiable from the repository: a solo build covering roughly 44 database tables across nine feature areas (tickets, orders, customers, incidents, SLA, automation, RBAC, reporting, and a separate customer portal); a documented, present-tense-honest test state (39/39 unit tests passing, 12 e2e specs, and 7 of 22 Vitest component tests currently failing — independently re-run and confirmed); and measured Lighthouse performance scores of 95 (desktop) and 68 (mobile) against the /login page on the same local, unoptimized next dev server instance used for the screenshots below — a production next build/next start audit was not performed and would likely score differently, and the desktop run followed a prior page visit (from screenshot capture) so it measured a warm Turbopack route-compilation cache rather than a cold first load.
heroTail: built and documented solo, including the gaps.

testing.ciSummary: No CI/CD pipeline exists — no .github/workflows, no vercel.json, no Dockerfile — so every test script runs locally, on demand, with nothing blocking a merge or push on failure. Independently re-run this session: 39/39 unit tests pass; 7 of 22 component tests fail, but not for one uniform reason — 1 failure (register-page.test.tsx) asserts on stale UI text ("Google sign-in (coming soon)") left over from before Google OAuth was wired up, while the other 6 (all of login-page.test.tsx) fail because the page now calls useSearchParams() to read a ?verified= flag and the test's next/navigation mock doesn't provide it, so the component throws during render. docs/TESTING.md already documents both causes separately; neither is a regression in the pages themselves.

retrospective: The most honest thing to say about OpsDesk's current state is that its own documentation caught real problems while being written and re-confirmed independently this session: 7 of 22 component tests fail, for two distinct reasons — one assertion on UI copy that no longer exists after Google OAuth replaced a disabled placeholder button, and six more because a later change added a useSearchParams() read that the tests' router mock never accounted for — so a green npm test badge would have been actively misleading if I hadn't run it and looked. That, combined with Row Level Security covering only 3 of roughly 44 tables (everything else depends on every query remembering an organization_id filter) and there still being no CI to catch either problem automatically, are the two things I'd want fixed before calling this production-ready: get a CI pipeline actually running on push, and either extend RLS as a real second line of defense or write down app-layer-only isolation as a deliberate, accepted trade-off instead of an implicit one. (Draft — for the portfolio owner to revise into a first-person reflection.)

_needsInput.reason: depends on ziadhatem.dev's existing case-study sequence
depends on which other case study should follow this one
no live deployment exists anywhere in the repo (no vercel.json, no confirmed hosting) — decide whether the hero CTA should point at the GitHub source instead, or be hidden entirely
could not confirm via `gh` (not installed in this session) whether github.com/ziad-hatem/OpsDesk is public or private — confirm before publishing a link to it

used the most recent commit's month (2026-07); the repo has no live launch date since status is in-development — confirm whether shippedDate should instead mean first-commit month (2026-03) or something else entirely for a not-yet-shipped project

measured against `npm run dev` (Turbopack, unminified) on localhost:3500/login: 95 desktop / 68 mobile. This is a large swing from an earlier same-week measurement of 76 on the same unoptimized dev server — the difference traces to the desktop run following a warm-cache page visit (from screenshot capture) versus a cold Turbopack compile earlier — neither number reflects a production `next build`/`next start`, which would likely score higher and more consistently than either dev-server run. Re-run against a production build if a stable, publishable number is wanted.

several architectureDecisions/testing/retrospective entries here cite real, currently-unresolved weaknesses (RLS gaps, JWT fallback-secret reuse, missing rate limiting) documented in the repo's own docs/SECURITY.md and independently re-confirmed in code this session (lib/server/passkey-assertion.ts, lib/server/mfa-assertion.ts, lib/server/mfa-email-auth.ts for the secret fallback; app/api/auth/passkey/lookup, app/api/passkey/authenticate/{start,finish}, app/api/public/status/[slug] for the missing rate limiting) — confirm these are acceptable to make public in a portfolio before this ships

_evidence.shippedDate: git log oldest→newest commit dates: first commit 2026-03-02 ("Initial commit from Create Next App"), most recent 2026-07-06 ("chore: add comprehensive documentation suite and update README"); shippedDate uses the latter
_evidence.contributors: git shortlog -sn --all: 21 commits as "Ziad Hatem", 4 as "ziad-hatem" — both <ziadhatem2022@gmail.com>, i.e. a single contributor across all 25 commits
_evidence.technologies/stackList: package.json dependencies/devDependencies + next.config.ts, eslint.config.mjs, playwright.config.ts, vitest.config.ts, tailwind (postcss.config.mjs) — independently re-diffed this session by a dedicated verification agent: every stackList entry maps to a real dependency, no major dependency (auth, payments, DB, UI, testing, state) is missing from the list
_evidence.testing.unitTestCount: grep -rE "^\s*(test|it)\(" across tests/unit (39 matches, 11 files) + tests/components (22 matches, 5 files); independently re-run this session with `npm run test:unit` and `npm run test:components` (env vars sourced from .env.local, matching docs/TESTING.md's documented procedure) — 39/39 unit pass, 15/22 component pass with the 7 failing cases named individually and their two distinct root causes confirmed by reading login-page.test.tsx, register-page.test.tsx, and the corresponding page source
_evidence.testing.e2eTestCount: grep -rE "^\s*test\(" tests/e2e --include=*.ts → 12 matches across 2 spec files (login.spec.ts, register-verify.spec.ts); re-confirmed this session
_evidence.testing.ciSummary: docs/TESTING.md "Current suite health" and "No CI" sections; confirmed no .github/, vercel.json, or Dockerfile exist in the repo root, re-confirmed this session via ls -la at repo root
_evidence.no CI/license/deploy config: no .github/ directory, no vercel.json, no netlify.toml, no CNAME, no LICENSE*, no .env.example; package.json has no "homepage" field
_evidence.architectureDecisions: docs/DECISIONS.md ADR-001, ADR-002, ADR-003, ADR-004, ADR-007 (condensed into trade-off form; document states these are reconstructed post-hoc from code, not original team ADRs, and all are marked Status: Inferred). Independently re-verified this session line-by-line against docs/DECISIONS.md by a dedicated agent — all 5 entries fully grounded, near-verbatim in several places, no exaggeration found
_evidence.measuredQuality.a11yDetail: package-lock.json entries for eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y and axe-core (both transitive, not in package.json); grep -o "jsx-a11y/[a-z-]*" node_modules/eslint-config-next/dist/index.js listed exactly 6 enabled rules (re-confirmed this session); grep -rl "aria-[a-z]+=" app --include=*.tsx found 8 files — note: a naive plain-substring `grep -rl "aria-"` returns 19 files because it also matches Tailwind variant-selector class names like `aria-invalid:`/`aria-selected:`, not real JSX ARIA attributes, so the stricter `aria-[a-z]+=` pattern is the correct one and is what produces the 8-file count used here
_evidence.lighthouseScore: This session: npx lighthouse http://localhost:3500/login --preset=desktop (score 95: LCP 1.5s, TBT 0ms, CLS 0, FCP 0.3s, SI 0.4s) and default mobile preset (score 68: LCP 8.3s, TBT 330ms, CLS 0, FCP 1.1s, SI 1.2s), both against `npm run dev` on a port dedicated to this audit; dev server stopped immediately after both runs completed. Raw reports saved to lighthouse/desktop-report.json and lighthouse/mobile-report.json
_evidence.roleRows/status: README.md "Known Limitations" and "Missing Information" sections explicitly state no CI/CD, no LICENSE, and no live/demo URL exist
_evidence.verification: All claims above were independently re-checked this session by three parallel read-only verification passes (tests/CI/RLS counts; ADR/security grounding; stack-list/a11y diff) run against the current repo state rather than trusted from the original draft. No material discrepancy was found in any of the three passes — only the nuances already folded into the fields above (component-test dual root cause, a11y grep pattern specificity, ~44-table figure sourced from docs/SECURITY.md rather than the ADR text itself).
